title: Jerk chicken
subtitle: Jamaican allspice and scotch bonnet chicken
excerpt: Jerk chicken at its best is deeply spiced and properly charred: bone-in thighs marinated in allspice, scotch bonnet, thyme, and garlic, then cooked slowly and finished over high heat. The marinade goes on at least a day ahead so the spices work all the way to the bone.
sourceNotes:
• Helen Willinsky, *Jerk: Barbecue from Jamaica* (1990). The standard published authority on the allspice-forward marinade and the cooking technique over pimento wood.
• Levi Roots, *Reggae Reggae Cookbook* (2008). Cross-reference for a British-Caribbean home kitchen adaptation.
• Riaz Phillips, *Belly Full: Caribbean Food in the UK* (2021). Used for context on the UK Caribbean community's home versions and the grill-oven technique as a charcoal substitute.

recipe.batchNotes: The jerk marinade can be made in double quantity and frozen. Marinate and freeze raw chicken pieces in sealed bags with the marinade; defrost overnight in the fridge.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The chicken must be marinated for a minimum of 8 hours. Overnight is better; 24 to 48 hours gives the fullest flavour. The longer it sits, the more the allspice and scotch bonnet penetrate.

glossaryTerms.definition: The Jamaican spice marinade and cooking technique built around allspice (pimento) and scotch bonnet chillies. Traditionally cooked over green pimento wood in a drum; at home, reproduced with an oven and hot grill finish.

content.text: from most chilli marinades is allspice. The spice is named for the way it reads as cinnamon, clove, and black pepper simultaneously, and in a jerk marinade it gives the background warmth that scotch bonnet alone can't. You want both: the floral heat of the chilli and the deeper resonance of the allspice.

Bone-in thighs are the cut for this dish. The marinade needs time — plan on 24 hours in the fridge if you can manage it. A food processor blitzes the marinade together in under a minute; a pestle and mortar takes longer but bruises the ingredients rather than pureeing them, which gives a slightly different texture to the finished crust.

Without a barbecue, the closest result comes from a hot oven followed by a close grill finish. The grill char is not optional — it is what moves the dish from oven chicken with a spiced glaze to something that reads as properly cooked jerk.

items.prepNote: deseeded for medium heat; keep seeds for hot

content.text: A food processor is the quickest route to a smooth marinade paste. A deep roasting tin holds the chicken flat during oven cooking. You need a grill (overhead in the oven or a freestanding model) that can get close to the chicken for the final char.

Put the scotch bonnet, {{allspice}} of allspice, thyme leaves from {{thyme}}, {{garlic}} of garlic, {{ginger-root}} of ginger, {{soy-sauce-dark}} of dark soy sauce, juice of 1 lime, {{dark-brown-sugar}} of dark brown sugar, {{vegetable-oil}} of vegetable oil, {{cinnamon-ground}} of cinnamon, and {{black-pepper}} of black pepper into a food processor. Blitz to a coarse paste. Taste: it should be fragrant, fiery, and balanced with the dark soy. Season with salt.

Score {{chicken-thigh}} chicken thighs deeply through to the bone with a sharp knife — 3 cuts per thigh. Push the marinade into the cuts and under the skin, then coat the whole surface. Cover and refrigerate for at least 8 hours. Remove from the fridge 45 minutes before cooking.

Heat the oven to 200°C (fan 180°C). Arrange the thighs in a single layer in a roasting tin, skin side up. Roast for 35 to 40 minutes until the juices run clear when the thickest thigh is pierced. The skin will be darkening but not yet charred.

Preheat the grill to its highest setting. Move the roasting tin to within 8 cm of the element and grill for 5 to 8 minutes until the skin blisters and chars in patches. Watch closely: the sugars in the marinade burn fast. You want char marks, not ash. Rest for 5 minutes, then serve with lime wedges.

items.cause: Not enough allspice, or the chilli seeds were removed, reducing heat.
items.fix: Add another teaspoon of allspice and the seeds from one scotch bonnet. Allspice is the flavour backbone — the amount matters.

items.symptom: Chicken skin is pale after roasting
items.cause: The marinade was wiped off before cooking, or the oven was too cool.
items.fix: Leave a generous coating of marinade on the skin before it goes in. The grill step does most of the charring, but the oven needs to start the browning process.

items.symptom: Chicken burnt under the grill before cooking through
items.cause: The grill was started before the oven step was complete.
items.fix: The oven step is not optional. The grill only charred the surface; the interior must reach a safe temperature first. Always finish the oven stage before grilling.

items.cause: Scotch bonnet seeds were left in, or three chillies were used.
items.fix: Deseed the chillies before blitzing, and start with one chilli rather than two. The heat level is adjustable at the marinade stage.

content.text: Jerk is associated with Boston Bay in Portland parish, Jamaica, where roadside pits have sold charcoal-cooked pork and chicken since the early twentieth century. In the UK it arrived with Caribbean communities in the 1950s and 1960s and is now made in home kitchens and takeaway shops across the country. The style sold from market stalls in Brixton and Shepherd's Bush often uses the same spice ratios as the Jamaican original; the key variable is the heat source. At home, the oven-plus-grill method gets close enough that the dish remains clearly itself.
